summary:
The child has pre-existing immunity from previous complete rabies vaccinations, eliminating the need for passive immunoglobulin (RIG). Post-exposure prophylaxis should consist solely of additional rabies vaccine administration—typically given as two doses at Day 0 and Day 3—as per established guidelines by CDC and WHO.

Agent1's reasoning (Round 2): \boxed{\text{D}}  
After reviewing all sources again including updated versions of CDC/WHO protocols, I confirm my original analysis stands correct. Prior completion of full rabies vaccination renders any use of RIG unnecessary due to active humoral memory against virus. In addition, while there may be variations across regions or specific exposures, global consensus supports administering only rabies vaccine upon re-exposure—not more than necessary—and most practical regimens involve minimal dosages such as two initial doses spaced over three days. Therefore, choice D remains optimal based on available data.

Final answer: $\boxed{\text{D}}$

Only summarize Agent1’s reasoning without adding external information.

The child has prior full rabies vaccination, which confers protective immunity and makes passive immunoglobulin (RIG) unnecessary. According to CDC and WHO guidelines, post-exposure treatment requires only two doses of rabies vaccine administered at Days 0 and 3.  

$\boxed{\text{D}}$
